nativeRange.habitat: Soft, slightly acidic freshwater streams with leaf litter, submerged wood, and biofilm-covered rocks; the modern Crystal Red itself is a selectively-bred red color morph first developed in Japan in the 1990s from the wild Bee Shrimp parent stock.

decorPreferences: dense moss (Java moss, Christmas moss, Fissidens)
driftwood and leaf litter (Indian almond, oak)
RO/DI water remineralized with shrimp-specific salts

diet.notes: Generally regarded as a grazing omnivore that subsists primarily on biofilm, soft algae, and decaying plant matter. Has been documented accepting shrimp-specific pellets, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach), Indian almond leaves, and occasional protein foods. Calcium- and mineral-supplemented foods are commonly recommended to support successful molting. Tend to be slower and more deliberate feeders than Neocaridina, so feedings should be small and easily consumed within a few hours to avoid water-quality decline.

goodWith: small peaceful nano fish that ignore shrimp (chili rasboras, ember tetras, pygmy corydoras)
avoidWith: Neocaridina species (different water-parameter preferences; will not interbreed but compete)

summary: A small, selectively-bred freshwater dwarf shrimp from the Caridina cantonensis Bee/Crystal complex, prized for striking red-and-white banding. Generally considered intermediate to keep — has been reported to require soft, acidic, low-TDS water and stable parameters.
careNotes: Foundation entry covering the entire Caridina cantonensis Bee/Crystal complex. Common morphs include Crystal Red (CRS), Crystal Black (CBS), wild Bee / Red Bee / Black Bee, Golden Bee, Snow White, Tiger Bee, Tangerine Tiger, Pinto, Galaxy Pinto, Blue Bolt, Shadow Panda, King Kong, and Wine Red. Within Crystal Red and Crystal Black lines, grades S, SS, SSS, and Mosura (or SSS Mosura) reflect increasing white coverage and band opacity, with Mosura being the most prized. Taxonomy note: some sources (notably the Logemann description) have reassigned the modern selectively-bred Crystal/Bee lines to Caridina logemanni, while most hobby sources and Wikipedia retain Caridina cantonensis (often written as Caridina cf. cantonensis) — both attributions are in active use and the entry preserves cantonensis as the more commonly cited binomial. All complex members share similar husbandry and will interbreed with each other; do not mix with Neocaridina (different parameter preferences) or Sulawesi Caridina. Generally advised to use RO/DI water remineralized with shrimp-specific GH/KH+ salts to hit low TDS (typically reported 120-180 ppm) and soft, slightly acidic conditions; active soil substrates (Akadama-style buffering soils) are commonly used to hold pH in the 6.2-6.8 range. Highly sensitive to copper, ammonia, nitrite, and parameter swings — avoid copper-based medications and snail treatments, and only add to fully cycled, mature tanks (sources commonly suggest 2-3+ months of cycling before introducing colonies). Cross-source notes on parameters: Flip Aquatics cites 20-23°C and pH 6.6-6.9; Fishkeeping World extends to 21.7-25°C and pH 5.8-7.4; AquariumBreeder cites optimal 21-23°C with broader 6.2-7.2 pH; Wikipedia notes wild Bee parent stock at 21-26°C. Final ranges are averaged with a bias toward the soft-and-acidic consensus characteristic of this species. Fishkeeping World rates these as 'intermediate' and AquariumBreeder calls them a 'medium / high-order shrimp' — selected intermediate over Flip's 'entry-level' framing (which refers to within the Caridina-keeping niche, not the wider hobby). The 10 US gal (~38 L) minimum from Fishkeeping World and AquariumBreeder was rounded to 40 L per the project's nice-value tank-size convention.
breedingNotes: Has been reported as moderately challenging compared to Neocaridina — colonies will breed on their own in well-tuned tanks but are sensitive to parameter shifts, TDS spikes, and substrate exhaustion. Females reach sexual maturity around 4-4.5 months and carry 20-30 fully-formed shrimplets ('berried') for roughly 30 days; broods emerge as miniature adults with no larval stage. Stable soft, acidic water with consistent low TDS (commonly 120-180 ppm), mature biofilm, and abundant moss cover for shrimplets to graze are the most-cited keys to colony success. Mixing different complex morphs produces unpredictable offspring that often revert toward wild-bee patterning over generations; line-bred colonies are typically kept morph-pure.

primary.name: Flip Aquatics
primary.notes: Primary trade-source collection page; species-specific product page (https://flipaquatics.com/products/crystal-red-shrimp) yielded the canonical parameter set: temperature 20-23°C (68-74°F), pH 6.6-6.9, GH 5-7, KH 0-2, TDS 130-160; grading scale C through SSS.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on access; substituted with Fishkeeping World and AquariumBreeder. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Secondary substitute (Shrimp Farm 403'd). Cross-confirmed adult size ~3 cm, lifespan 18-24 months, pH 5.8-7.4, temperature 21.7-25°C, family Atyidae, intermediate care level, copper sensitivity, peaceful temperament, 10-gallon minimum, omnivore diet.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check. Confirmed adult size 2.5-3 cm, lifespan 1-2 years, 10 gal (~40 L) minimum, pH optimal 6.6-6.9 (range 6.2-7.2), GH 3-7, KH 0-4, TDS 100-250, medium / 'high-order shrimp' difficulty, sexual maturity 4-4.5 months, broods of ~30 shrimplets every 5-6 weeks.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Caridina cantonensis)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback for family Atyidae / order Decapoda, native range Taiwan / Hong Kong / Southern China, color morph catalog (Black Bee, Blue Bee, Golden Bee, Crystal Red, Princess Bee, Blue Bolt, Tangerine Tiger, Shadow Panda), and confirmation that Crystal Red is a selectively-bred line first developed in Japan from the wild Bee parent stock since 1996.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally reported to molt every 3-6 weeks, with younger shrimp molting more frequently than adults. Discarded exoskeletons are typically left in the tank and re-consumed for calcium recovery; they should not be removed. Insufficient mineral content (low GH, low calcium, low TDS below ~100 ppm) is frequently associated with the 'white ring of death' incomplete-molt fatality. Parameter stability — particularly stable TDS and GH — is widely considered the most important molting factor, more so than for Neocaridina.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Breeds in stable soft, acidic tanks but is notably more sensitive to parameter swings than Neocaridina. See top-level breedingNotes for details.
